1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
88	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
89	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
90	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
91	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
92	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
93	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
94	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
95	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
100	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
101	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
102	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
103	supplied.
104	
105	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
106	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
107	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
108	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
109	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
110	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
111	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
112	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
113	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
114	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
115	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
117	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
118	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
120	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
121	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
122	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
123	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
124	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
125	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
126	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
127	avoids. The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
129	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
130	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
131	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
132	
133	\section{Mathlib API survey}
134	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
135	
136	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
137	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
138	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
139	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
140	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
141	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
142	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
143	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
144	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
145	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
146	source.
147	
148	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
149	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
150	monoidal-category instance
151	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
152	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
153	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
154	\[
155	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
156	\]
157	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
158	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
159	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
160	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
161	% SOURCE QUOTE:
162	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
163	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
164	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
165	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
166	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
167	%   ...
168	%   tensorUnit := unit _
169	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
170	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
171	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
172	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
173	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
174	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
175	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
176	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
177	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
178	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
179	
180	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
181	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
182	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
183	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
184	the whiskering-stability data:
185	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
186	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
187	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
188	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
189	% SOURCE QUOTE:
190	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
191	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
192	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
193	%  ...
194	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
195	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
196	%  ...
197	% noncomputable instance :
198	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."
199	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
200	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
201	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
202	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
203	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
204	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
205	hypothesis chain.
206	
207	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
208	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
209	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
210	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
211	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
212	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
213	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
214	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
215	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
216	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
217	% SOURCE QUOTE:
218	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
219	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
220	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
221	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
222	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
223	%     intro Φ
224	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
225	%     infer_instance)"
226	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
227	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
228	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
229	\(A\).
230	
231	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
232	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
233	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
234	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
235	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
236	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
237	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
238	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
239	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
240	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
241	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
242	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
243	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
244	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
245	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
246	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
247	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
248	critical path.
249	
250	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
251	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
252	is already present in Mathlib: the file
253	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
254	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
255	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
256	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
257	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
258	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
259	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
260	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
261	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
262	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
263	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
264	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
265	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
266	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
267	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
268	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
269	
270	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
271	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
272	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
273	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
274	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
275	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
276	independently of any upstream timing.
277	
278	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
279	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
280	
281	\begin{definition}
282	
283	\leanok
284	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
285	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
286	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
287	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
288	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
289	  \[
290	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
291	  \]
292	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
293	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
294	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
295	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
296	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
297	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
298	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
299	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
300	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
301	  canonical compatibility
302	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
303	     \;\cong\;
304	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
305	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
306	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
307	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
308	
309	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
310	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
311	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
312	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
313	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
314	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
315	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
316	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
317	\end{definition}
318	
319	\begin{lemma}
320	
321	\leanok
322	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
323	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
325	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
326	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
327	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
328	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
329	  \[
330	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
331	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
332	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
333	  \]
334	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
335	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
336	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
337	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
338	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
339	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
340	  \[
341	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
342	    \qquad
343	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
344	  \]
345	  the associator
346	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
347	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
348	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
349	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
350	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
351	\end{lemma}
352	
353	\begin{proof}
354	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
355	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
356	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
357	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
358	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
359	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
360	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
361	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
362	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
363	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
364	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
365	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
366	\end{proof}
367	
368	\begin{remark}
369	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
370	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
371	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
372	        lem:whisker_of_W}
373	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
374	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
375	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
376	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
377	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
378	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
379	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
380	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
381	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
382	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
383	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
384	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
385	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
386	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
387	
388	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
389	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
390	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
391	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
392	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
393	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
394	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
395	  fixed-base presheaves in
396	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
397	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
398	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
399	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
400	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
401	  it.
402	
403	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
404	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
405	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
406	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
407	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
408	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
409	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
410	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
411	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
412	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
413	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
414	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
415	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
416	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
417	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
418	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
419	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
420	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
421	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
423	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
424	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
425	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
426	\end{remark}
427	
428	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
429	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
430	
431	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
432	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
433	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
434	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
435	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
436	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
437	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
438	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
439	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
440	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
441	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
442	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
443	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
444	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
445	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
446	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
447	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
448	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
449	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
451	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
452	to the off-path route~(e).
453	
454	\begin{lemma}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
458	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
459	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
460	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
461	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
462	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
463	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
464	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
465	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
466	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
467	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
468	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
469	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
470	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
471	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
472	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
473	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
474	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
475	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
476	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
477	  \[
478	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
479	      \;\;:=\;\;
480	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
481	  \]
482	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
483	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
484	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
485	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
486	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
487	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
488	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
489	\end{lemma}
490	
491	\begin{proof}
492	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
493	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
494	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
495	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
496	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
497	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
498	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
499	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
500	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
501	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
502	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
503	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
504	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
505	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
506	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
507	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
508	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
509	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
510	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
511	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
512	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
513	
514	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
515	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
516	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
517	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
518	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
519	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
520	\end{proof}

522	\begin{lemma}
523	
524	\leanok
525	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
526	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
528	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
529	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
530	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
531	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
532	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
533	  morphism
534	  \[
535	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
536	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
537	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
538	  \]
539	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
540	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
541	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
542	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
543	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
544	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
545	\end{lemma}
546	
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
549	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
550	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
551	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
552	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
553	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
554	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
555	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
556	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
557	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
558	  isomorphism}
559	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
560	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
561	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
562	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
563	  therefore extension of scalars along
564	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
565	
566	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
567	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
568	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
569	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
570	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
571	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
572	  the abstract pullback functor.
573	
574	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
575	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
576	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
577	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
578	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
579	   \;\cong\;
580	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
581	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
582	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
583	  comparison
584	  \[
585	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
586	      \;\longrightarrow\;
587	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
588	      \;\otimes\;
589	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
590	  \]
591	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
592	
593	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
594	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
595	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
596	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned
597	  commit. The closure passes through two presheaf-level ingredients, of which
598	  exactly one now remains open.
599	
600	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
601	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
602	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
603	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
604	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
605	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
606	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
607	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
608	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
609	  \otimes_S N)\).
610	
611	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
612	  \[
613	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
614	  \]
615	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
616	  presheaf-level adjunction
617	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
618	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
619	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
620	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
621	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
622	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
623	  composing the resulting comparison with
624	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
625	
626	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
627	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
628	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
629	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
630	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
631	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
632	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
633	  presheaf level. The depth of this sub-chain is at present uncertain.
634	
635	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law: the
636	  direct gluing construction of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
637	  invokes it (twice per cover member) to descend to the canonical presheaf-level
638	  associator. Closing the H1 residual is therefore the sole remaining substrate
639	  obligation.
640	
641	  \emph{The make-or-break: only the free-cover case is on the critical path.} The
642	  statement above is proved here for \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
643	  \(M, N\), and in that generality the closure passes through the H1 residual
644	  (\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)), whose
645	  presheaf-level building blocks are Mathlib-absent. But the associator
646	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes this comparison only on a \emph{trivialising
647	  cover}, where \(M\) and \(N\) restrict to \emph{free} modules. On that free cover
648	  the comparison is the elementary, structural one: sheaf-\(\otimes\) coincides with
649	  presheaf-\(\otimes\) on the free pieces, and restriction along the open immersion is
650	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{ring isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\),
651	  so the isomorphism follows from
652	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} alone, with \emph{no} appeal to the
653	  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of the
654	  construction is therefore the free-cover specialisation of this lemma, to be
655	  discharged \emph{without} H1; the arbitrary-module statement (with its H1 residual)
656	  is strictly stronger than the group law requires. As a fallback, should the
657	  free-cover case itself genuinely require the general adjunction H1, the by-hand
658	  route would have gained nothing over instantiating the coherent monoidal structure,
659	  and one would revert to comparing against the superseded route~(e)
660	  \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
661	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}).
662	\end{proof}

664	\begin{lemma}
665	
666	\leanok
667	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
668	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
669	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
670	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
671	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
672	  product: the canonical map
673	  \[
674	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
675	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
676	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
677	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
678	  \]
679	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
680	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
681	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
682	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
683	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
684	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
685	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
686	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
687	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
688	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
689	\end{lemma}
690	
691	\begin{lemma}
692	
693	\leanok
694	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
695	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
696	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
697	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
698	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
699	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
700	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
701	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
702	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
703	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
704	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
705	\end{lemma}
706	
707	\begin{proof}
708	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
709	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
710	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
711	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
712	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
713	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
714	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
715	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
716	\end{proof}

729	\begin{lemma}
730	
731	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
732	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
733	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
734	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
735	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
736	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
737	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
738	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
739	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
740	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
741	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
742	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
743	  left-whiskered morphism
744	  \[
745	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
746	  \]
747	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
748	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
749	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
750	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
751	
752	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
753	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
754	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
755	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
756	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
757	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
758	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
759	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
760	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
761	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
762	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
763	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
764	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
765	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
766	\end{lemma}
767	
768	\begin{proof}
769	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
770	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
771	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
772	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
773	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
774	
775	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
776	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
777	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
778	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
779	  \[
780	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
781	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
782	  \]
783	
784	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
785	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
786	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
787	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
788	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
789	
790	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
791	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
792	  linear maps (Mathlib
793	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
794	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
795	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
796	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
797	
798	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
799	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
800	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
801	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
802	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
803	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
804	\end{proof}

816	\begin{lemma}
817

819	[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]

822	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
823	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
824	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
825	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
826	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
827	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
828	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
829	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
830	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
831	  isomorphism:
832	  \[
833	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
834	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
835	  \]
836	  This is a closed go/no-go bridge --- it converts ``\,a morphism lies in
837	  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''. Under route~(e) the

839	  retained as a supplement rather than as the associator's load-bearing step.

842	\begin{proof}
843	  This is a one-morphism reading of the structural identity that the
844	  sheafification of presheaves of \(R_0\)-modules \emph{is} the localization of
845	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R_0\) at the class
846	  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
847	  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
848	  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the

850	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.inverseImage\_W\_toPresheaf\_eq\_inverseImage\_%

884	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API

887	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the

892	\paragraph{Two-tier strategy.} The group law is assembled by a \emph{two-tier}
893	strategy, of which this route~(e) is the second tier. The \textbf{primary} tier
894	builds the commutative group directly on isomorphism classes of \emph{locally
895	trivial} (invertible) sheaves (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring
896	Mathlib's ring-level Picard group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) /

904	\(\mathtt{Skeleton}\), and in particular never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation

923	\begin{lemma}
924	
925	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
926	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
927	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_germ, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_bijective_of_isIso, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}
928	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
929	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
930	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
931	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
932	  \[
933	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
934	  \]
935	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
936	  characterised on germs by
937	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
938	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
939	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
940	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
941	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
942	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
943	
944	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
945	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
946	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
947	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
948	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
949	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
950	\end{lemma}
951	
952	\begin{proof}
953	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
954	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
955	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
956	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
957	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
958	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
959	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
960	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
961	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
962	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
963	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
964	\end{proof}

1119	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1120	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1121	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1122	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1123	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1124	\end{proof}

1127	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1128	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1129	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1130	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
1131	This block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be
1132	formalized; its Lean declarations are being removed.

1134	\begin{lemma}
1135	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1136	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1137	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W}
1138	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1139	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1140	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1141	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1142	  \(J.W\):
1143	  \[
1144	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1145	    \qquad
1146	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1147	  \]
1148	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1149	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1150	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1151	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1152	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1153	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1154	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1155	\end{lemma}
1156	
1157	\begin{proof}
1158	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1159	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1160	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1161	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1162	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1163	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1164	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1165	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1166	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1167	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1168	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1169	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1170	\end{proof}

1172	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1173	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1174	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1175	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1176	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1177	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1178	associator is now built directly by
1179	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1180	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized;
1181	no Lean declaration realizes it and none should.

1183	\begin{lemma}
1184	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1185	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1186	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1187	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1188	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1189	  instance
1190	  \[
1191	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1192	  \]
1193	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1194	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1195	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1196	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1197	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1198	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1199	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1200	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1201	  category
1202	  \[
1203	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1204	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1205	  \]
1206	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1207	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1208	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1209	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1210	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1211	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1212	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1213	  composites.
1214	
1215	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it is currently
1216	  \emph{stated-but-unformalized} (no Lean declaration realizes the
1217	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance yet), pending the sole open obligation
1218	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1219	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1220	\end{lemma}
1221	
1222	\begin{proof}
1223	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1224	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1225	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1226	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1227	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1228	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1229	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1230	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1231	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1232	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1233	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1234	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1235	  at any point.
1236	\end{proof}
1237	
1238	\begin{lemma}
1239	
1240	\leanok
1241	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1242	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1244	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1245	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1246	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1247	  \[
1248	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1249	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1250	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1251	  \]
1252	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1253	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1254	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1255	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1256	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1257	  ever required.
1258	
1259	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1260	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1261	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1262	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1263	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1264	\end{lemma}
1265	
1266	\begin{proof}
1267	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1268	  The isomorphism is assembled by \emph{gluing canonical local isomorphisms}. The
1269	  construction rests on \emph{no} whiskering of the sheafification localizer,
1270	  \emph{no} instance \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and \emph{no} stalk
1271	  computation.
1272	
1273	  Fix a common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\) (for instance an affine open cover).
1274	  On each member \(U\) form the canonical composite
1275	  \[
1276	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1277	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1278	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1279	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1280	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1281	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1282	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1283	  \]
1284	  in which:
1285	  \begin{itemize}
1286	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1287	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1288	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1289	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1290	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1291	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1292	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1293	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1294	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1295	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1296	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1297	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1298	  \end{itemize}
1299	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1300	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1301	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1302	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1303	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1304	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1305	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1306	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1307	  composites agree on the overlap.
1308	
1309	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1310	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1311	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1312	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1313	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1314	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1315	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1316	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1317	  associator.
1318	
1319	  The single non-formal input is the restriction-compatibility comparison
1320	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf associator
1321	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules internal-hom gluing
1322	  are already available. Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1323	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1324	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1325	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1326	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1327	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1328	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1329	  are not consumed.
1330	
1331	  \emph{The free-cover make-or-break.} The group law of
1332	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} consumes this associator only for
1333	  \emph{locally-trivial} modules (line bundles), so the cover \(\{U\}\) above may be
1334	  taken to \emph{trivialise} them: on each \(U\) the modules \(M, N, P\) restrict to
1335	  \emph{free} \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules. This is the whole point of building the
1336	  associator by gluing rather than by instantiating a coherent monoidal category. On a
1337	  free cover the two applications of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
1338	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} that each local composite consumes are needed
1339	  \emph{only} in the cheap, structural case: where the modules are free,
1340	  sheaf-\(\otimes\) coincides on \(U\) with presheaf-\(\otimes\) (sheafification is the
1341	  identity on the already-sheafy free pieces), so the comparison is the elementary
1342	  base-change isomorphism of free modules along the open immersion, requiring no
1343	  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of this
1344	  construction is therefore the free-cover specialisation of
1345	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, established \emph{without} the Mathlib-absent
1346	  general adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)
1347	  (``H1'', see \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). As a fallback, should that
1348	  free-cover restriction-compatibility genuinely require H1, the by-hand associator
1349	  has not in fact gotten smaller, and one would instead obtain associativity by
1350	  comparing against the superseded route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to
1351	  \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}).
1352	\end{proof}
1353	
1354	\begin{lemma}
1355	[Left and right unitors for $\otimes_X$]
1356	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1357	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
1358	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

1360	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With
1361	  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the
1362	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
1363	  \[
1364	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M,
1365	    \qquad

1371	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1372	  The presheaf monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) supplies
1373	  the left and right unitors \(\lambda, \rho\) at the presheaf level. Their
1374	  sheafifications \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\lambda)\),
1375	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\rho)\), composed with the sheafification
1376	  counit identifying the sheafified structure-sheaf presheaf with
1377	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the step already used in the structure-sheaf-unit
1378	  comparison \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) in the substrate file), give the
1379	  two isomorphisms. This route is the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern and uses no
1380	  abstract pullback.
1381	\end{proof}

1406	\begin{lemma}
1407	
1408	\leanok
1409	[Inverse of an invertible module]

1426	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

1445	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1446	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction

1454	  \]

1475	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)

1513	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1514	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1515	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1516	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1517	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an
1518	  off-critical-path equivalence; the units-group packaging is
1519	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)
1520	\end{proof}

1522	\begin{lemma}
1523	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1524	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1526	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1527	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1528	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1529	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1530	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1531	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1532	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1533	  functor
1534	  \[
1535	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1536	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1537	  \]
1538	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1539	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1540	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1541	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1542	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1543	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1544	  canonical isomorphism
1545	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1546	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1547	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1548	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1549	\end{lemma}
1550	
1551	\begin{proof}
1552	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1553	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1554	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1555	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1556	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1557	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1558	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1559	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1560	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1561	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1562	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1563	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1564	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1565	  same cover.
1566	\end{proof}

1583	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1584	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1703	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules

1707	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1708	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1709	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1710	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1711	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1712	  01CR).
1713	
1714	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1715	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1716	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1717	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1718	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1719	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1720	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1721	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1722	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1723	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1724	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1725	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1726	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1727	
1728	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1729	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1730	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1731	  \[
1732	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1733	    \quad
1734	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1735	    \quad
1736	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1737	    \quad
1738	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1739	  \]
1740	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1741	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1742	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1743	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1744	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1745	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1746	
1747	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1748	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1749	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1750	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1751	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1752	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1753	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1754	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1755	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1756	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1757	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1758	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1759	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1760	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1761	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1762	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1763	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1764	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1765	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1766	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1767	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1768	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1769	  pinned commit.
1770	
1771	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1772	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1773	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1774	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1775	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1776	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1777	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1778	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1779	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1780	
1781	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1782	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1783	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1784	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
1785	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
1786	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
1787	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
1788	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
1789	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
1790	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
1791	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
1792	  already supplies.
1793	\end{lemma}

1898	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1899	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1900	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1901	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1902	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1903	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1904	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1905	  \[
1906	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1907	  \]
1908	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1909	  abelian-group structure with addition
1910	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1911	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1912	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1913	  inverse
1914	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1915	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1916	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1917	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1918	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1919	  map
1920	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1921	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1922	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1923	\end{theorem}
